title: The Government
overview: The United Kingdom is a parliamentary democracy where government is chosen by the people through elections. The Prime Minister leads the government, and the Cabinet and Civil Service help make and carry out policies. Power is also shared with local councils and the devolved governments of Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.

study_sections.heading: Government Structure
study_sections.content: UK is parliamentary democracy: voters elect MPs to represent them. Government formed by political party with most MPs in House of Commons. PM is Head of Government and leads Cabinet. Cabinet includes senior ministers who head departments (Defence, Education, Health). Civil Service supports government by providing expert, neutral advice and carrying out policy. Opposition questions and challenges Government's work.

study_sections.heading: Local and Devolved Government
study_sections.content: Local government (councils) provides local services: housing, planning, transport, education, waste collection. Each area has elected councillors making local decisions. Devolution gives powers to governments in Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland. Scottish Parliament (Edinburgh) controls health, education, justice, transport. Welsh Parliament/Senedd (Cardiff) controls health, education, environment, culture. NI Assembly (Belfast) controls health, education, agriculture, justice.

study_sections.heading: Elections and Voting
study_sections.content: General elections held at least every 5 years. UK uses first-past-the-post voting—candidate with most votes in each constituency becomes MP. To vote must be: 18 or over, British/Commonwealth/Irish citizen living in UK, registered to vote. Register at www.gov.uk/register-to-vote. Can stand for election if 18+ and British/Irish/qualifying Commonwealth citizen. Voting at polling stations, by post, or by proxy. Electoral Commission oversees fair elections and campaign funding.

key_facts: The UK Government runs the country, creates policy, and implements laws passed by Parliament.
The UK is a constitutional monarchy and parliamentary democracy, so the government is accountable to Parliament and the public.
The Prime Minister is Head of Government and is appointed by the monarch as the leader able to command a Commons majority.
The Prime Minister chairs the Cabinet, represents the UK at home and abroad, oversees policy, and advises the monarch on key appointments and royal powers.
Around 20 senior ministers form the Cabinet, each responsible for a government department such as foreign affairs, defence, health, or education.
Collective responsibility means Cabinet ministers support agreed decisions in public.
The Chancellor of the Exchequer manages national finances and taxation, while the Home Secretary oversees internal affairs and security.
The Foreign Secretary leads diplomacy and represents the UK internationally.
Junior ministers and parliamentary under-secretaries support departmental work.
The Civil Service provides impartial expertise and implements government policy with integrity and honesty.
The Opposition, led by the Leader of the Opposition, proposes alternatives and scrutinises government decisions.
Parliament holds the government to account through debates, select committees, and question times, including weekly Prime Minister’s Questions.
The Monarch’s Speech at the State Opening of Parliament sets out the government’s legislative programme.
Devolved governments in Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland manage areas such as education, health, and transport within their nations.
Local government delivers services like housing, planning, and waste management across communities.
The UK Government represents the nation in bodies such as the UN, NATO, and the Commonwealth and must keep the confidence of the House of Commons.
Losing a vote of confidence can trigger a general election, reinforcing accountability and respect for democratic processes.
memory_hook: Picture government as a steering wheel—Prime Minister and Cabinet guide, Parliament checks the direction, and civil servants keep the motion smooth.

options: Senior ministers responsible for major departments
Carry out government policy & provide impartial advice
Services like housing, planning, education, transport
Transfer of powers to regional governments

questions.prompt: How often must elections be held?

options: In person, by post, or by proxy
Allows citizens to choose who governs
